speaker: interviewer
text: Today is December 12, 1995. This is an interview with Tamara Weinreich-- W-E-I-N-R-E-I-C-H, nee Buchman, B-U-C-H-M-A-N. I'm Rona Arato-- A-R-A-TO. We're in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, and the interview will be in English. Today's December 12, 1995. This is an interview with Tamara Weinreich, nee Buchman. I'm Rona Arato. We're Toronto, Ontario, Canada, and the interview will be in English. Would you please state your name, the date of your birth, and where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Tamara Weinreich. And I was born in Warsaw, in 1930. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was Tamara, and they called me also Tosha.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name-- your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My last name-- maiden name was Buchman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-U-C-H-M-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents' names were Israel. They call him [NON-ENGLISH]. And my mother's name was Pola or [NON-ENGLISH] They call her Pola.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's name is Jacob, but they called him [NON-ENGLISH] or [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was born in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your home.

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents got married in Warsaw. My mother comes from a family that she used to help them out during the-- before the war and before she got married. And my father also was born in Warsaw and also had to help out his parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we lived on [NON-ENGLISH], in Warsaw. And across the street where we lived, in an apartment building, we had our business. The business was called [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was a business in partnership with a Polish guy who gave the money, because my parents didn't have money. And it was selling plywood in veneer. My mother was actively involved. She was responsible for the veneer, and my father for the plywood. And all the carpenters who were coming there, they knew my mother very well as the business lady there too. But until they came to this stage, which was about three years before the war started, they had very hard, hard life. My mother was sewing [NON-ENGLISH]-- was sewing, let's say, underwear and thing like undershirts. And then she has-- had to bring it to stores on her own, on her back. And she was doing it like in such a way that nobody will know that she is doing it, that she is sewing and bringing it to the stores. She was telling her family that she is studying, and she is well off. And-- but she was really hard-working. Nighttime, she was sewing. I could see her sewing on the machine and running there early in the morning to the store to bring the-- the materials ready, the products ready. My father was a carpenter by profession, and he also was working very hard. He was working at home doing the wooden part for sofas-- for somebody, naturally. So they both worked very hard. And we were two kids. They had to support us. And my mother was extremely energetic, and she pushed my father into business. And this how they met this Polish guy who gave the money. And they opened the business. So three years-- last years before the war-- we were very well off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your apartment where you lived. What was-- how big was it? What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- [LAUGHS] --it depends in which stage. When my parents were married, they had only one room. Then-- then they-- when we came into being, we had two rooms and a like a salon. And we had a-- a bedroom now. How we sleep, where we slept, I-- I really don't remember-- and a small kitchen. And in this, my mother was sewing. My father was working on carpentry. But as I said, when they did the business, then they had already an apartment-- a larger apartment. I think it was a larger salon, a larger bedroom. I think also one bedroom the same, and a bigger kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish. You know, the assimilation was big in Poland. And even so, my mother comes from a religious home-- very religious. And my father comes from a religious home. Still they-- they-- in order to probably to put themselves in the life of Poland and make a-- a living, they had to know Polish. They had to perfect this Polish more. And-- and they wanted their children to advance and study, and therefore, they spoke to us Polish. Amongst themselves, from time to time, they spoke Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have extended family in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had-- my mother comes from a family of eight-- four brothers and four sisters. One of the brothers, when he was very young, he ran away from the-- from Poland. He was a communist. He wanted, you know, a-- a better life for people. And he had to run away. And he landed in Argentina. As a matter of fact, I have a cousin, his son, who lives now in Israel, in Kibbutz Hakuk. His name is Simcha Wigotsky. My mother from home, her name was Wigotsky. Now, all the other brothers and sisters lived in Warsaw, and I met them. And I still remember them. They were-- the names of her sister was Ida, then Esther-- [NON-ENGLISH], they called her-- then my mother was-- and-- I missed-- ah, and Manya, who was during the war, also with us. Now if she has-- the brothers were-- the three brothers in Warsaw was Moshe, Meyer, and the last one I hardly even-- Smulik, I think. Also, we had, from my mother's side, my grandfather. My grandmother's name was Freidle. She comes from [NON-ENGLISH]. And her maiden name was [NON-ENGLISH]. And she had a sister in Warsaw. Her name was Golda Amsterdamer-- married with children. During a certain time when probably my mother was about 18, she lived with this aunt, because it was very hard for my grandmother and grandfather to, you know, to raise eight children. She lived there a short while-- I don't know how long-- at this [NON-ENGLISH]-- is Aunt [NON-ENGLISH]-- in her house, because they were well-off people. They had a factory of-- of-- [LAUGHS] --of combs. But the combs were made of galalith. It's a form of plastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. How would you describe your family's religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I want to say also about my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's family, there was my grandfather. My grandfather, he was born in Staszow, and he came from Staszow here. He had four children-- no-- four-- four son and a daughter-- five children. The youngest was a daughter. My grandmother died. Her name was Tama, the same like mine. And he remarried and had with this other woman-- her name was [NON-ENGLISH]-- his name was Chaim-- Chaim Buchman-- and he had another four children-- two boys and two girls. The boys' name was Itzhak and Mendel, and the girls who were younger, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH], who was in my-- my age. Now, my grandfather was also a carpenter, and he worked very hard. And he had a place on a [NON-ENGLISH]. It was an apartment-- 1/2 was apartment where he lived-- a room-- a one room, really, with a kitchen. And he lives there. And the other part, another big room is his place where he work on carpentry. My father helped a lot. After he got married, he was helping this grandfather. He was bringing us very often there. Now, my father had four brothers from his-- like, his-- my grandfather's first wife. He had four brothers, and the brother's name was Joseph, who later on lived in Belgium, and married, and had a son, Uri, who lives now in Israel. He had another brother, Hersch, another brother Ephraim, and a sister, Shosha. And this sister's daughter lives in Rishon, in Israel. She has children and married. She was with us all the time until we came to Israel. And during the war, her parents were with us as well. About the religious-- religious part. Now, my parents, as I said, came from religious homes. But when they married, they rather were traditional. That on Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur they were going to-- to synagogue, but otherwise, not. They had to work, both of them, on Saturdays. And-- but when they-- they were very respectful to their parents. And whenever the parents came to the house, we had a special kosher set of dishes. And the parents ate on those dishes. My-- my mother's father was a scribe-- was a Hasid. And he was mostly, instead to attend to the business, which he had, they had also a business. We-- we cannot call it business. They were working at home on-- on making buttons also from this galalith-- which was a platform of plastic-- making buttons. The sons were helping. And my grandfather had a big beard. He was a lovely, very full of humor person. That's what they were telling me. He rather would study Gemara, study Chumash, than a Torah, and scribe in the Torah, because he was a Torah scribe. And this has to be done in calligraphy-- cannot be printed, the Torah. And my grandmother was this energetic and working hard. My mother, when she was at home, also she had to help very much. My mother was extremely energetic, and she was-- she taught herself how-- how to sew by opening a pillow, opening a sheet, and sewing by herself the-- the pieces, of-- buying material and sewing. And later on, she even opened sort of a school at home, and children-- young girls were coming to learn from her sewing. This way she support the younger sisters and brothers and also helped the parents as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went when-- in my grade one, my parents send me to Hebrew school. And the name of the school was Tarbut. And I had a-- even a girlfriend whom I remember. And her name was Hela Weisbrod-- that she was coming with me together to this-- to this school.

speaker: interviewer
text: From grade one, from this time, I remember once that a teacher who taught us embroidery-- one day I forgot the embroidery. And I was sewing only a paper, which was-- [LAUGHS] --printed there. And she came to me and said, how come you doing? And I was-- I started to cry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This I remember. I remember when they asked me, you know, probably how much is one and one, and I was looking at the ceiling. We had some decorations, and I put the decorations together in order to answer the question. That's what I remember grade one. Grade two, I was already send. Probably my parents didn't have enough money to pay for private school. So I was sent to-- to a public school, which is a Jewish public school, probably. I don't remember exactly. But we spoke Polish. And I remember that we had to pray. And the Jewish children, I think, were leaving the class when the prayers were. I don't remember really very much. So the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall any anti-Semitism at school or with the other children?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the area of Warsaw that you lived in, was this a Jewish area or was it a mixed area?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived on a border between the Jewish area and non-- so-called non-Jewish area-- like-- Jew-- you know. And this was on [NON-ENGLISH]. And you know, next to us was [NON-ENGLISH]. And from this street up, there mostly Jewish people live.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what the-- what were the relations like between the Jews and the non-Jews in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I played across the street where we had the business. At the-- behind the building there were-- they-- there were carriages, and horses, and carriages. And especially my brother, who is younger than me-- two years younger-- he was playing on-- in this carriage-- sitting on it and playing like he is the coachman. And he took me also. I was rather shy more. And we were sitting there, and other children, probably from the street, played with us. I remember one of the games that we played. When a coachman came and put the horses to the coach and started to go, we ran after the coach-- my brother first. I was after him. And we jumped at the back of the coach so the coachman won't see us. And we went like this. And the other children would-- would call him. Hey, coachman! Hit them! Hit them! Then we ran away. This was our play.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the Passover at your home. How-- how did you celebrate Passover?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a seder-- having seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I don't think we ever had seders, or I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And any other holiday celebrations? Did you attend a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember-- don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were your parents' customers? Did they deal with everybody in-- Jewish and non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly, as we are-- we were mostly in a non-Jewish area, rather, on the border. I imagine that most of the carpenters were non-Jewish people. Because during the war, we were hiding in a place in this area. We were staying in a place in this area. And my mother had to sew for herself a special hat, a woman's hat, naturally, with a big black veil. Like the Polish people, if somebody died in the family, they were-- they were wearing such a veil. So he-- she sewed for herself this kind of veil. And she had to walk in the street. So whenever a streetcar passed by, she was afraid that one of the Polish carpenter who knew her from the-- from the business might recognize her and give her into the hand of the Germans. So she always, when the streetcar pass, puts a veil around her face, sort of. So I know that most of them probably were non-Jewish carpenters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall from the years prior to the war? Did you recall hearing about what was happening outside of Poland or outside of Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a little girl, and I didn't. I didn't hear nothing, you know, from this kind. I was too little, probably, to-- I remember when the war-- let's say-- I remember when the war started, OK-- that they were calling on the ram calls. They were saying in Polish, they say [POLISH], which means, watch out-- attention, attention. A plane is coming. You know? To-- the German planes started to come. Later on they bombed. So that's what I remember, really. From outside this-- the-- this city, I-- I don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- what was your parents' attitude at that time? Do you remember them being worried, or was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before I start with this, I would like also to say about my-- my grandparents, if I may.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, please do.

speaker: interviewee
text: Could I?

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am going back to my grandparents, my mother's parents, that my mother took good care of her parents even in later years when we were better off. My grandfather had an asthma, and my mother, together with her sister, rented a-- a house, a villa, not far from-- from Warsaw. And it's call-- it's called, the place, Otwock. It's like a resort place more. And the last two summers, I was at my grandparents' place for summer-- for summer vacation. And I loved to come there, because my grandfather was the scribe, as I mentioned, writing the Torah. And he taught me calligraphy-- Hebrew calligraphy. And when I came to Israel, I didn't know Hebrew, but I knew how to write the Hebrew letters in calligraphy. And another thing-- my grandfather, every morning, he told me, oh, good morning. Jump from bed. We'll say now the prayer, which was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And he taught me to pray. And why do I mention it? Because during the war, when I was on the Aryan side with the Poles, I-- I had to-- I-- I was under assumed-- different name. And I-- I had to act as a Polish girl, as a non-Jewish girl, as a Christian one.

speaker: interviewee
text: But every morning, I stood before the window and I said, [NON-ENGLISH] in Hebrew, so nobody will hear-- only me. And I said this they cannot take away from me. So-- and besides, I remember this time as very lovely. My grandmother, she was wearing a wig-- wig, naturally. They were very Orthodox. And she-- oh-- Friday night, she was making the challahs and cookies. And she gave me the cookies with milk every morning. And I loved them. And then when she prepared everything, I helped her to do a little challah. We brought it in the cellar. There used to be a cellar, because there were not Frigidaires in this time. So she put everything in the cellar. And my grandfather made wine from raisins, like for Friday night. And then on Friday night, we brought everything up. And we had the fish, the gefilte fish, like real gefilte fish, with the gefilte things in the fish itself. And so I have lovely, lovely memories from this time from my grandfather. That's why I wanted to mention. And also, my parents-- my father was very respectful to his family. Very often-- ah-- I have to come back. My grandmother and grandfather from my mother's side, before they left for this villa in Otwock, they lived in Warsaw on a street called [NON-ENGLISH], which means lucky street. It wasn't so lucky for them, naturally, but very often, my mother was taking us there to play, to be with the grandparents. And I-- later on, I painted a picture from this house. Because I remember there were two beds with-- covered with a red plush cover. In between this was a little-- a little cupboard with a clock, probably alarm clock, on it. And it was making click-clock, click-clock all night through. And when I was sleeping at the grandparents, they slept each one in a different bed, naturally. And this-- this little table was in between. I could hear this clock running here, and, you know, ticking all the time, and those red covers off during the daytime. When they put there, I always remember. Now my grandfather-- my-- my father always used to take us to his father, and the children, and the stepmother. And I loved to come there, because there was a girl my age-- my father's stepsister. And beside, I love to clean up there, because they had the-- the place of work and the-- and where they lived together-- sort of-- only divided with a divider. There was full of sawdust in the house. And when my stepmother probably didn't have time, and was untidy, so I always were tidying up, making the bed, doing the sink. I loved doing this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my mother always says, oh, why do you take them there? It's so untidy. But my father said, no, no. He loved to come there with us. And we liked it, too-- me and my brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why I came back to-- to talk about grandparents. But now coming back, you asked me what was the attitude of my parents. To--

speaker: interviewer
text: Or the adults around you. Do you remember people being worried, or-- was--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. As the war, you know, broke out, I remember only my-- my mother-- if nay, probably she knew that the war is inevitable. What she did is she was a very extremely smart woman, extremely energetic. And because of her we are alive. Me, and my brother, and my father survived. Because she decided, probably, she heard during the First World War that they didn't have textiles, and she bought textiles-- big, big pieces of textiles. And she rented a room in the same apartment that we lived. And she filled up this room with textiles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. We're just going to pause for a moment, and then we'll continue.
